title: SEGMENT 12 — Engineered phages: when natural phages aren't enough

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Engineered phages: when natural phages aren't enough

The next frontier, which is where a lot of current academic and biotech work is happening, is engineering phages — modifying their genomes to give them properties their natural counterparts don't have.
The natural phage library is enormous, but it has gaps.
Sometimes no natural phage exists with the right host range for a particular pathogen.
Sometimes the available phages are lysogenic and you need them to be lytic.
Sometimes you want to add a payload — a CRISPR system, an antibiotic-resistance-reversal cassette, an immune-evasion modification.
Engineering closes the gaps.
The first and most important kind of engineering is lysogenic-to-lytic conversion.
As we discussed, lysogenic phages aren't useful for therapy — they integrate quietly and don't kill the host.
But sometimes the best phage you have against a particular pathogen is naturally lysogenic, and you'd like to convert it.
The conversion is conceptually simple: knock out the lysogeny gene — the one encoding the repressor, the CI equivalent — and the phage loses its option to integrate.
It can only do lytic.

The Spencer cystic fibrosis case at Great Ormond Street in 2019 used exactly this — a phage that was naturally lysogenic against Mycobacterium abscessus, knocked out for its lysogeny gene by Graham Hatfull's lab at Pittsburgh, so that the engineered phage was obligately lytic and clinically useful.
The second kind of engineering is host range modification.
The tail fibers of a phage — the proteins that recognize the bacterial surface — determine which species and strains the phage can infect.
If you swap the tail fiber genes between phages, you can in principle swap host ranges.

This has been done experimentally with several phages — the Lu lab at MIT, the Marraffini lab at Rockefeller, the Voigt lab — and the dream is to be able to "program" a phage's host range by choosing tail fibers from a parts library.
Edit the phage's host range — make it kill the bacteria you want, not its natural prey.
That's the analogy.
We're not there yet for arbitrary host ranges, but the field is moving fast.
The third kind of engineering is CRISPR-armed phages.
This is a particularly clever idea pioneered by Tim Lu's lab at MIT and others.
You take a phage and load its genome with a CRISPR-Cas system targeted at the bacterial chromosome — for instance, targeted at antibiotic resistance genes.
When the phage infects the bacterium, the CRISPR machinery activates and cleaves the bacterial chromosome at the target sites.
The bacterium dies not from the phage's normal lytic program but from CRISPR-mediated chromosomal destruction.

And critically, the CRISPR can be programmed to specifically target resistance genes or virulence genes, so even if the phage doesn't kill the bacterium outright, it strips it of its resistance, re-sensitizing it to antibiotics.
The phage becomes a delivery vehicle for an antibacterial CRISPR weapon.
Locus Biosciences is one company commercializing this concept.
The fourth kind of engineering is payload addition for therapeutic purposes other than killing.
Phages can be loaded with genes that produce antibiotics on entry — the phage delivers an antibiotic gene to the host, which expresses it and kills itself.
Or genes that disrupt biofilm matrix — the phage isn't just killing the bacterium, it's degrading the protective biofilm that surrounds chronic infections.
Or genes that quench quorum sensing, preventing the bacterial population from coordinating virulence behaviors.
The phage becomes a chassis for various antibacterial payloads, not just a self-replicating virus.
There's also work on engineering phages as gene-delivery vehicles for therapeutic purposes other than antibacterial therapy.
We touched on this briefly in the transposons lecture, and it overlaps with the AAV and adenovirus delivery discussions.
Phages can be engineered to deliver genes to mammalian cells, although this is much more experimental than their use against bacteria.
Filamentous phages, in particular — M13, fd, f1 — have been explored for delivering DNA payloads to tumors and to other mammalian targets.
The advantages are that phages don't naturally replicate in mammalian cells, so there's no risk of unintended infection, and they can carry larger payloads than some viral vectors.

The disadvantages are that they're not naturally good at entering mammalian cells, so they need surface modifications — display of cell-targeting peptides, fusion to nanoparticles — to get efficient uptake.
The application is still niche, but it exists.
And finally, you can engineer phages to display antigens as vaccines.
We'll come back to this in Segment 13.
The general theme is that phages have, by now, become an engineering substrate as much as a natural resource.
You don't have to take the phage as you find it.
You can edit it, retool it, weaponize it differently.

The toolkit for phage engineering — CRISPR-based phage genome editing, recombineering, in vitro assembly of phage genomes from synthetic DNA — has matured to the point where building a custom phage with desired properties is a months-long lab project, not a years-long heroic effort.
